tracks.title: Python Core
tracks.description: The basics juniors often underestimate: variables, typing, truthiness, slicing, and naming.

lessons.title: Variables and Assignment
lessons.short_description: Understand what variables are, how assignment works, and what reassignment means.

content.title: Variables and Assignment
content.goal: By the end of this lesson, you should understand what variables are in Python, how assignment works, what reassignment means, and how to explain this clearly in a junior interview.
image_prompts: A cozy kitchen shelf with jars of jam. Each jar has a paper label: flavor, price, cups. One label is being moved from one jar to another.
Two drink cups. First cup has label drink → latte, then the label is moved to another cup: drink → cappuccino.
A café receipt showing: drink, cups, price per cup, total. Use labels like Python variable names.

sections.title: Tasty Metaphor
paragraphs: Imagine a kitchen full of jars.
Each jar contains something: strawberry jam, honey, cinnamon, chocolate cream.
A variable is like a label on a jar.
This means: put the label flavor on the value "strawberry".
In Python, a variable is not exactly a box. It is more like a name tag attached to an object.
If we write flavor = "strawberry" and then flavor = "chocolate", the label flavor moves.
Now it points to "chocolate".
The old strawberry jam is not inside the variable. The label simply no longer points to it.

items.title: Memory hook
items.content:
variable = name
assignment = attaching a name to a value
reassignment = moving the name to another value

sections.title: Short Theory
paragraphs: A variable is a name that refers to a value.
Here, tea refers to a string, cups refers to an integer, price refers to a float, and is_hot refers to a boolean.
The symbol = means assignment.
It does not mean “equals” like in math. It means: take the value on the right and assign it to the name on the left.
Read dessert = "cake" as: dessert now refers to "cake".
Not: dessert is mathematically equal to cake.
Python is not doing algebra here. It is doing label management. A tiny kitchen manager with strong opinions.
sections.code:
tea = "green tea"
cups = 3
price = 4.5
is_hot = True

dessert = "cake"

sections.title: Main Code Example
paragraphs: First, drink refers to "latte" and price refers to 5.
Then both variables are reassigned.
So Python prints the newest values.
sections.code:
drink = "latte"
price = 5

print(drink)
print(price)

drink = "cappuccino"
price = 6

print(drink)
print(price)

sections.title: Interview Spot
paragraphs: These are the kinds of questions a junior developer may hear in an interview.
Try to answer clearly and simply. The goal is not to sound fancy. The goal is to show that you understand what Python is actually doing.

items.title: Question 1: What is a variable in Python?
items.content: A variable in Python is a name that refers to an object or value. It does not store the value directly like a physical box; it points to an object in memory.

items.title: Question 2: What does assignment mean?
items.content: Assignment means binding a name to a value. In x = 5, the name x now refers to the integer object 5.

items.title: Question 3: What happens when you reassign a variable?
items.content: The variable name starts referring to a new value. The old value is not changed by reassignment; the name simply points somewhere else.

items.title: Question 4: Can one value have several variable names?
items.content: Yes. Several names can refer to the same object.

sections.title: Trap Zone
paragraphs: These are common beginner mistakes.
They look small, but they show whether you really understand assignment. So yes, tiny traps, big consequences. Python is polite, but it will still throw an error.

items.title: Trap 1: Reading assignment backwards
items.content: The variable name must be on the left. The value must be on the right.

items.title: Correct version
items.content: This is the correct order: the name cookies receives the value 10.

items.title: Trap 2: Using a variable before assignment
items.content: Python reads code from top to bottom. At the moment of print(smoothie), the name smoothie does not exist yet.

items.title: Correct version
items.content: Create the variable first, then use it.

items.title: Trap 3: Thinking reassignment changes the old value
items.content: favorite got the value "pie" before dessert was reassigned. Later, dessert moved to "cake", but favorite still refers to "pie".

sections.title: Practice
paragraphs: Now check whether the idea really landed.
Try to answer first. No peeking. The Python snake sees everything.

items.title: 1. Choose the Correct Explanation
items.content:
What does this code mean?

A) Python checks if tea and "mint" are equal
B) Python assigns the value "mint" to the name tea
C) Python creates a function called tea
D) Python prints "mint"

items.title: 2. Reassignment
items.content:
What happens here?

A) snack contains both values
B) snack now refers to "muffin"
C) Python gives an error
D) "cookie" becomes "muffin"

items.title: 3. Predict the Output
items.content:
What is printed?

A) vanilla, then caramel
B) caramel, then caramel
C) vanilla, then vanilla
D) Error

items.title: 4. Copying a Value
items.content:
What is printed?

A) 2
B) 5
C) cups
D) Error

items.title: 5. Updating a Variable
items.content:
What is printed?

A) 10
B) 3
C) 13
D) Error

items.title: 6. Find the Mistake
items.content:
What is the problem?

A) cake is used before assignment
B) Strings cannot be printed
C) Variable names cannot contain the letter c
D) The code is correct

items.title: 7. Assignment Direction
items.content:
What is the problem?

A) Numbers cannot exist in Python
B) The assignment is backwards
C) apples must be uppercase
D) There is no problem

items.title: 8. Better Variable Name
items.content:
Which name is better for storing the number of coffee cups?

A) x
B) coffee_cups
C) thing
D) data

items.title: 9. Boolean Variable Name
items.content:
Which name is better for storing whether a user is active?

A) active_user_status_now_yes
B) is_active
C) a
D) something

items.title: 10. Coding Task
items.content: Create variables for a small café order. Create drink with the value "green tea", cups with the value 2, price_per_cup with the value 3, create total_price, and print: Order: green tea, cups: 2, total: 6
items.code:
drink = "green tea"
cups = 2
price_per_cup = 3

# create total_price here

# print the result here

sections.title: Say It Like in an Interview
paragraphs: Try to explain this aloud before revealing the answer.

items.title: What is a variable in Python, and what happens during assignment?
items.content: A variable in Python is a name that refers to a value or object. Assignment binds a name to a value. For example, in x = 10, the name x refers to the integer 10. If we later write x = 20, the name x is reassigned and now refers to 20.

rows: A name that refers to a value
Binding a name to a value
Making the name refer to a new value

lessons.title: Mutable vs Immutable Objects
lessons.short_description: Learn why some Python objects can change and others cannot.

content.title: Mutable vs Immutable Objects
content.goal: By the end of this lesson, you should understand the difference between mutable and immutable objects in Python, recognize common examples, and explain why this topic matters in junior interviews.
image_prompts: A kitchen table with a reusable recipe notebook and a sealed printed recipe card.
Two baskets: one with fruits that can be rearranged, labeled list, and one sealed jar labeled tuple.
content.completion_body:
You’ve sorted the soft salad bowls from the fixed recipe cards.

Mutable and immutable objects are no longer mysterious little kitchen goblins.

Take a breath, save your progress, and get ready for the next Python bite.

sections.title: A bowl you can change, a card you cannot
paragraphs: Imagine a kitchen.
Some foods can be changed after you prepare them.
A salad is flexible. You can add tomatoes, remove onions, or mix in cheese.
That is like a mutable object.
Other foods are fixed once they are made.
A printed cookie recipe card cannot be changed directly. If you want a different version, you create a new recipe card.
That is like an immutable object.
In Python, some objects can be changed in place.
Other objects cannot be changed in place. When you “change” them, Python actually creates a new object and moves the variable name to it.

items.title: Memory hook
items.content:
Mutable = same bowl, ingredients can change.

Immutable = new recipe card, old one stays the same.

sections.title: Short Theory
paragraphs: In Python, every value is an object.
Some objects are mutable. This means their contents can be changed after the object is created.
Examples of mutable objects:
list
dict
set
Some objects are immutable. This means their contents cannot be changed after the object is created.
Examples of immutable objects:
int
float
str
bool
tuple
The important thing is this:
Reassignment is not the same as mutation.
When you reassign a variable, the name starts pointing to another object.
When you mutate an object, the object itself changes.
This difference is very important when several variables refer to the same object.

items.content:
In the first example, the string "cake" is not changed into "pie". The name dessert simply starts pointing to a new string.

In the second example, the list itself is changed. The same list now contains one more item.

Examples of mutable objects:
•	List
•	Dict
•	set

data.markdown:
In Python, every value is an object.

Some objects are **mutable**. This means their contents can be changed after the object is created.

*Examples of mutable objects:*

- list
- dict
- set

Some objects are **immutable**. This means their contents **cannot be changed** after the object is created.

*Examples of immutable objects:*

- int
- float
- str
- bool
- tuple

The important thing is this:
**Reassignment is not the same as mutation.**

- When you **reassign** a variable, the name starts pointing to another object.

- When you **mutate** an object, the object itself changes.

This difference is very important when several variables refer to the same object.

In the first example, the string **`"cake"`** is not changed into **`"pie"`**. The name `dessert` simply starts pointing to a new string.

In the second example, the list itself is changed. The same list now contains one more item.

sections.title: Main Code Example
sections.code:
dessert = "cake"
print(dessert)

dessert = dessert + " with cream"
print(dessert)

toppings = ["cheese", "tomato"]
print(toppings)

toppings.append("basil")
print(toppings)

items.content:
What happened here?

The string "cake" was not edited inside. Python created a new string: "cake with cream".

But the list toppings was edited in place. The same list received a new ingredient: "basil".

So, strings behave like fixed recipe cards.

Lists behave like salad bowls. You can throw in more basil and pretend it was your plan all along.

data.title: Your mission:
data.markdown: Watch the difference between **reassigning a string** and **mutating a list**.

data.title: Python
data.code:
dessert = "cake"
print(dessert)

dessert = dessert + " with cream"
print(dessert)

toppings = ["cheese", "tomato"]
print(toppings)

toppings.append("basil")
print(toppings)

data.markdown:
**What happened here?**

The string **`"cake"`** was not edited inside. Python created a new string: **`"cake with cream".`**

But the list `toppings` was edited in place. The same list received a new ingredient: **`"basil"`**.

So, strings behave like fixed recipe cards.

Lists behave like salad bowls. You can throw in more basil and pretend it was your plan all along.

data.title: Visual recap
data.markdown:
The jam jar is like a **string**: when the value changes, Python gives the name a new object. 
The lunchbox is like a **list**: the same container can receive another item.

data.title: Memory hook
data.markdown:
**Reassignment** moves the name.

**Mutation** changes the object.

sections.title: Interview Spot
paragraphs: In interviews, this topic often appears in simple-looking questions.
But the interviewer is not only checking whether you know the words “mutable” and “immutable”.
They want to see whether you understand what happens to objects in memory.

items.title: Question 1: What is a mutable object in Python?
items.content: A mutable object is an object that can be changed after it is created. For example, a list can be changed by adding, removing, or replacing elements.

items.title: Question 2: What is an immutable object in Python?
items.content: An immutable object is an object that cannot be changed after it is created. For example, strings, integers, floats, booleans, and tuples are immutable.

items.title: Question 3: What is the difference between mutation and reassignment?
items.content:
Mutation changes the object itself.

Reassignment changes what the variable name points to.

items.title: Question 4: Why can mutable objects be tricky?
items.content:
Mutable objects can be tricky because two variables can point to the same object.

If one variable changes the object, the other variable will see the change too.

sections.title: Trap Zone
paragraphs: Mutable and immutable objects are not difficult, but they are sneaky.
Like a jar of pickles at the back of the fridge: you think nothing is happening, and then suddenly everything smells like dill.

items.title: Trap 1: Thinking strings can be changed in place
items.content: Wrong mental model:
items.after_text:
This will cause an error because strings are immutable.

You cannot change one character inside a string.

items.title: Correct mental model
items.content: Create a new string:

items.title: Trap 2: Thinking reassignment changes the original object
items.content: Wrong mental model:
items.after_text:
This does not turn "cake" into "pie".

It only moves the name dessert to a new object.

items.title: Correct mental model
items.content: Think of reassignment as moving a label.
items.after_text:
Now dessert points to "pie".

The old string "cake" was not edited.

items.title: Trap 3: Copying a list by assignment
items.content: This looks innocent:
items.after_text: This happens because menu_a and menu_b point to the same list.

items.title: Correct mental model
items.content: Create a real copy:
items.code:
menu_a = ["soup", "salad"]
menu_b = menu_a.copy()

menu_b.append("pasta")

print(menu_a)
print(menu_b)

items.title: Trap 4: Thinking tuples are always fully unchangeable inside
items.content:
A tuple itself is immutable.

But if a tuple contains a mutable object, that inner object can still be changed.
items.after_text:
The tuple did not change its structure.

But the list inside the tuple was mutated.

Tiny Python gremlin moment. Legal, but suspicious.

items.title: Trap takeaway
items.content:
If the object is immutable, Python creates a new object.
If the object is mutable, Python may change the same object in place.

data.markdown:
Mutable and immutable objects are not difficult, but they are sneaky.

Like a jar of pickles at the back of the fridge: you think nothing is happening, and then suddenly everything smells like dill.

data.title: Trap 1: Thinking strings can be changed in place
data.afterMarkdown:
This will cause an error because strings are immutable.

You cannot change one character inside a string.

data.title: Trap 2: Thinking reassignment changes the original object
data.afterMarkdown:
This does not turn `"cake"` into `"pie"`.

It only moves the name `dessert` to a new object.

data.title: Correct mental model
data.markdown: Think of reassignment as moving a label.
data.afterMarkdown:
Now `dessert` points to `"pie"`.

The old string `"cake"` was not edited.

data.title: Trap 3: Copying a list by assignment
data.code:
menu_a = ["soup", "salad"]
menu_b = menu_a

menu_b.append("pasta")

print(menu_a)

# Output: ['soup', 'salad', 'pasta']
data.afterMarkdown: This happens because` menu_a` and `menu_b` point to the same list.

data.title: Correct mental model
data.code:
menu_a = ["soup", "salad"]
menu_b = menu_a.copy()

menu_b.append("pasta")

print(menu_a)
print(menu_b)

# Output
# ['soup', 'salad']
# ['soup', 'salad', 'pasta']

data.title: Trap 4: Thinking tuples are always fully unchangeable inside
data.markdown:
A tuple itself is immutable.

But if a tuple contains a mutable object, that inner object can still be changed.
data.afterMarkdown:
The tuple did not change its structure.

But the list inside the tuple was mutated.

Tiny Python gremlin moment. Legal, but suspicious.

data.title: Trap takeaway
data.markdown:
- If the object is **immutable**, Python creates a new object.
- If the object is **mutable**, Python may change the same object in place.

sections.title: Practice
paragraphs: Try to answer.
The goal is not to memorize. The goal is to predict what Python will do.

items.title: 1. Choose the Correct Explanation
items.content:
What does “mutable” mean?

A. The object can be changed after creation.
B. The object cannot be used in a variable.
C. The object is always a number.
D. The object disappears after printing.
items.output: A. The object can be changed after creation.

items.title: 2. Mutable or Immutable?
items.content:
Choose mutable items:

A. list
B. str
C. int
D. dict
E. tuple
F. set
G. bool

items.title: 3. Predict the Output
items.content:
What will be printed?

A. drink
B. DRINK
items.after_text: drink.upper() creates a new string, but the result is not saved anywhere.

items.title: 6. Find the Mistake
items.content:
Is this code correct?

A. Yes
B. No
items.after_text:
The mistake is that the code tries to change one character inside a string.

Strings are immutable, so this causes an error.

items.title: 7. Assignment Direction
items.content:
Which explanation is correct?

A. Put the name toppings on the list ["cheese", "tomato"].
B. Put the list inside the word toppings.
C. Change "cheese" into "tomato".
D. Compare toppings with the list.
items.after_text:
Assignment works from right to left.

Python first creates the list:

["cheese", "tomato"]

Then it binds the name toppings to that list.

items.title: 8. Better Variable Name
items.content:
Choose the best replacement for x.

A. menu_items
B. number
C. is_ready
D. cake
items.after_text:
menu_items clearly tells us that the variable stores several items from a menu.

Good variable names should explain the meaning of the value.

items.title: 9. Boolean Variable Name
items.content:
Choose the best improved version.

A. is_available
B. available_list
C. food
D. availability_name
items.after_text:
Boolean variables usually sound good with names like:

is_available
has_discount
can_order
is_empty

The name should sound like a yes/no question.

items.title: 10. Coding Task
items.content:
Create a list called pizza_toppings.

It should contain two toppings.

Then add one more topping using .append().

Print the final list.

data.question:
## 1. Choose the Correct Explanation

What does **mutable** mean?

options.text: The object can be changed after creation.
The object cannot be used in a variable.
The object is always a number.

data.explanation: A mutable object can be changed after it is created. Examples include `list`, `dict`, and `set`.

data.question:
## 3. Reassignment

What happens here?

options.text: The variable name dessert starts pointing to a new string.

data.explanation:
The variable name `dessert` is reassigned.

The string **`"cake"`** does not change. Strings are immutable.

The name `dessert` simply starts pointing to a new string: **`"pie"`**.

data.question:
## 4. Predict the Output

What will this code print?
data.explanation:
A list is mutable, so `.append()` changes the same list.

The final output is:

```python
['cookies', 'muffins', 'brownies']

data.question:
## 5. Copying a Value

What will this code print?
data.explanation:
`basket_a` and `basket_b` point to the same list.

So when we change the list through `basket_b`, we also see the change through `basket_a`.

Same salad bowl, two name tags. Python is not being dramatic here — just very literal.

The final output is:

```python
['apple', 'banana', 'orange']

data.question:
## 6. Updating a Variable

What will this code print?
data.explanation:
Strings are immutable.

`drink.upper()` creates a new string, but we did not save it anywhere.

So the original value is still:

```python
tea

data.question:
## 7. Find the Mistake

What is wrong with this code?

options.text: The code tries to change one character inside a string.
The variable name cake is not allowed.

data.explanation:
The mistake is that the code tries to change one character inside a string.

Strings are immutable, so this causes an error.

data.question:
## 8. Assignment Direction

Read this line and choose a correct explanation:

options.text: Put the name toppings on the list ["cheese", "tomato"].
Put the list inside the word toppings.

data.explanation:
Assignment works from right to left.

Python first creates the list: ["cheese", "tomato"]

Then it binds the name toppings to that list.

data.question:
## 9. Better Variable Name
Which variable name is better? Choose the best replacement for x.
data.explanation:
`menu_items` clearly tells us that the variable stores several items from a menu.

Good variable names should explain the meaning of the value.

data.question:
## 10. Boolean Variable Name

Which boolean variable name is better? Choose the best improved version.
data.explanation:
Boolean variables usually sound good with names like:

- is_available
- has_discount
- can_order
- is_empty

The name should sound like a yes/no question.

data.task:
## 10. Coding Task

Create a list called `pizza_toppings`.

It should contain two toppings.

Then add one more topping using `.append()`.

Print the final list.
data.starterCode:
# Create a list called pizza_toppings with two toppings


# Add one more topping using .append()


# Print the final list
data.explanation:
One correct solution is:

pizza_toppings = ["cheese", "tomato"]
pizza_toppings.append("basil")

print(pizza_toppings)

sections.title: Say It Like in an Interview
paragraphs: Try to explain this aloud before revealing the answer.

items.title: What is the difference between mutable and immutable objects in Python?
items.content:
Mutable objects can be changed after they are created. For example, lists, dictionaries, and sets are mutable. If I append an item to a list, the same list object is updated.

Immutable objects cannot be changed after creation. For example, strings, integers, booleans, and tuples are immutable. If I appear to change a string, Python actually creates a new string and the variable name is reassigned to it.

The main difference is that mutation changes the object itself, while reassignment changes what the variable name refers to.

rows: Moving a variable name to another object
Two variables point to the same object
Use .copy() for a simple list copy
Create a new string and reassign the variable
Mutation changes the object; reassignment changes the name binding
